icd_category: A04.7 — Enterocolitis due to Clostridium difficile
one_liner: A 72-year-old female presents with profuse watery diarrhea, crampy abdominal pain, and low-grade fever 5 days after completing a course of clindamycin for a dental abscess.

stage2.hpi_extension: The patient reports 4 days of profuse, watery, non-bloody diarrhea with 10-15 loose stools per day. The stools have a characteristic foul odor. She has crampy lower abdominal pain, low-grade fevers, and progressive weakness. Symptoms began 5 days after completing a 10-day course of clindamycin for a dental abscess. She was also hospitalized 3 weeks ago for a urinary tract infection and received IV ceftriaxone. She has poor oral intake and feels lightheaded when standing. She denies hematemesis, melena, or recent travel. She takes daily omeprazole for GERD.
stage2.pmh: GERD, hypertension, osteoarthritis, type 2 diabetes mellitus, recent UTI (hospitalized 3 weeks ago)
stage2.surgical_history: Right total knee replacement 2 years ago
stage2.medications: Omeprazole 40 mg daily, lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen PRN, metformin 1000 mg BID
stage2.social_history: Non-smoker. No alcohol use. Lives in an assisted living facility. Retired. No recent travel.
stage2.physical_exam: General: **Elderly female appearing fatigued and mildly dehydrated**. HEENT: **Dry mucous membranes, no oral lesions**. Cardiovascular: **Tachycardic, regular rhythm**. Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation. Abdomen: **Soft, diffuse tenderness greatest in the lower quadrants, mild distension, hyperactive bowel sounds, no rebound or guarding, no peritoneal signs**; **Rectal exam: Watery stool, guaiac negative**. Extremities: **Decreased skin turgor, no edema**.

lab_results.result: Positive for toxigenic C. difficile (toxin B gene detected)
lab_results.clinical_significance: Confirms C. difficile infection — PCR is highly sensitive for toxigenic strains

lab_results.result: Positive for toxin A and B
lab_results.clinical_significance: Positive toxin EIA with positive NAAT confirms active toxin-producing infection (not just colonization)
Marked leukocytosis (WBC >15,000 is a severity criterion); leukemoid reaction can occur in fulminant CDI
Acute kidney injury from dehydration, hypokalemia from diarrheal losses, hyponatremia
Mildly elevated from hypovolemia; markedly elevated lactate would suggest fulminant disease
Low albumin is a marker of severe CDI and poor prognosis
Elevated inflammatory marker consistent with active colitis

lab_results.result: No Salmonella, Shigella, Campylobacter, or E. coli O157:H7

lab_results.clinical_significance: Indicates inflammatory diarrhea consistent with CDI colitis

imaging_results.result: Diffuse colonic wall thickening (up to 8 mm) predominantly involving the sigmoid and descending colon with pericolonic fat stranding. Accordion sign present (contrast trapped between thickened haustra). No free air. No megacolon (maximal colonic diameter 5 cm).
imaging_results.clinical_significance: CT findings consistent with C. difficile colitis. No evidence of toxic megacolon or perforation.

imaging_results.result: Dilated loops of colon without megacolon. No free air under diaphragm. Thumbprinting of colonic wall.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Thumbprinting suggests mucosal edema; no toxic megacolon (dilation <6 cm).

stage3.key_tests_rationale: C. difficile NAAT is the most sensitive test for detecting toxigenic C. difficile. A two-step algorithm (GDH/NAAT followed by toxin EIA) helps distinguish colonization from active disease. CBC identifies leukocytosis (severity marker). BMP detects AKI and electrolyte derangements from diarrheal losses. Lactate elevation suggests severity.

stage5.final_clue: An elderly patient from an assisted living facility with recent antibiotic exposure (clindamycin and ceftriaxone), PPI use, and recent hospitalization — all major risk factors — presenting with profuse watery diarrhea, leukocytosis (WBC 22.5), AKI, positive this conditionicile NAAT and toxin EIA, and CT showing colonic wall thickening with Severity markers (WBC >15K, Cr >1.5x baseline) classify this as severe CDI.

debrief.classic_presentation: C. difficile colitis presents with watery, foul-smelling diarrhea (typically non-bloody), crampy abdominal pain, fever, and leukocytosis following antibiotic exposure. The highest-risk antibiotics are fluoroquinolones, clindamycin, and broad-spectrum cephalosporins. Risk factors include advanced age, hospitalization, PPI use, and immunosuppression. Severe disease may present with leukemoid reaction (WBC >30,000), toxic megacolon, and septic shock.
debrief.pathophysiology: Antibiotics disrupt the normal colonic microbiome, allowing Clostridioides difficile spores (acquired from the environment, especially healthcare settings) to germinate and proliferate. Toxigenic strains produce toxin A (enterotoxin) and toxin B (cytotoxin), which inactivate Rho GTPases in colonocytes, leading to disruption of the actin cytoskeleton, tight junction breakdown, epithelial cell death, and intense neutrophilic inflammation. This produces characteristic pseudomembranes (raised yellow plaques of fibrin, mucus, and inflammatory cells). The hypervirulent NAP1/BI/027 strain produces more toxin and binary toxin, causing more severe disease.
debrief.treatment_overview: Non-severe CDI (WBC <15K, Cr <1.5): Oral fidaxomicin 200 mg BID x 10 days (preferred) or oral vancomycin 125 mg QID x 10 days. Severe CDI (WBC >15K or Cr >1.5x baseline): Oral vancomycin 125 mg QID x 10 days or fidaxomicin. Fulminant CDI (hypotension, shock, ileus, megacolon): Oral vancomycin 500 mg QID + IV metronidazole 500 mg Q8H; consider vancomycin retention enemas if ileus; surgical consultation for colectomy. Stop the inciting antibiotic if possible. Recurrent CDI: fidaxomicin, vancomycin pulse-taper, or fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) after 2+ recurrences. Avoid antimotility agents.
high_yield_pearls: The three highest-risk antibiotics for CDI are clindamycin, fluoroquinolones, and third-generation cephalosporins — but virtually any antibiotic can cause it.
Metronidazole is no longer recommended as first-line for any severity of CDI per IDSA/SHEA 2021 guidelines — vancomycin or fidaxomicin are preferred.
Do NOT test for cure — repeat testing after treatment leads to false positives because NAAT can remain positive for weeks after successful treatment.
Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) is >90% effective for recurrent CDI and is recommended after 2 or more recurrences.
PPI use increases CDI risk by 1.5-2x — discontinue PPIs if not clearly indicated.
sources: 2021 IDSA/SHEA Focused Update Guidelines on Management of C. difficile Infection
McDonald LC et al., Clinical Practice Guidelines for CDI, Clinical Infectious Diseases 2018
Kelly CP, C. difficile Infection, NEJM 2015
